required.title: [Read Aloud] Stories that model social awareness
required.author: Tom Rath (age-appropriate edition) introduces the concept that our interactions affect others. *Each Kindness* by Jacqueline Woodson explores what happens when social cues are ignored.

required.title: [For Mentor] The Whole-Brain Child
required.author: Daniel J. Siegel and Tina Payne Bryson

competence_markers.description: The student can identify the mood of a room or group and adjust their behavior accordingly
The student calibrates volume to the environment without being reminded in most situations
The student observes before joining a group activity — watches, assesses, then enters appropriately
The student can name at least three nonverbal signals that indicate how someone is feeling
The student demonstrates awareness that different settings require different behavior and can articulate why
The student shows emerging timing awareness — beginning to choose when to speak, ask, or act based on the social moment
The student can describe the difference between two social environments and explain how their behavior should change between them

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you walked into a room and could tell something was happening.
narration_prompts.detail: How did you know? What did you notice?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between how you act at a playground and how you act in a library?
Describe a time you watched a group before joining in.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you notice? How did you decide when to join?

narration_prompts.prompt: How can you tell if someone is happy, sad, or angry without them saying anything?
narration_prompts.detail: What do their face, body, and voice tell you?

narration_prompts.prompt: When is a good time to ask for something? When is a bad time?
narration_prompts.detail: How do you tell the difference?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does it mean to "read the room"?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you give me an example of someone who read the room well, and someone who did not?

cross_connections.description: Listening is the auditory component of social calibration. The child who listens well is already practicing half of what 3.3.7 develops. Environmental awareness extends listening to the visual and spatial dimensions.
Reading others' body language requires the foundation built in 3.3.1 — the understanding that bodies communicate. Calibration adds the next layer: reading those communications in context.

Etiquette provides the behavioral code for different settings. Calibration determines which code applies right now. A child who knows table manners (3.3.5) and can read whether the meal is formal or casual (3.3.7) deploys the right behavior at the right level.
Knowing when to speak, when to listen, and when to enter a conversation depends on the calibration skills developed here.

Navigating unfamiliar social environments is a mild stress exposure that builds resilience. The child who learns to read new rooms comfortably develops social confidence that transfers to every new situation they encounter.

Environmental awareness in a social setting mirrors environmental awareness in a physical setting. The habit of scanning, observing, and assessing before acting is the same skill applied to different domains.

content.why_this_matters:
Have you ever walked into a room and immediately known that something was wrong — that people were upset, or that something exciting had just happened? You could feel it before anyone said a word. That ability — noticing what is happening around you before anyone explains it — is one of the most important skills a person can have.

Some children rush into every situation the same way. They run into a quiet room at full volume. They interrupt a serious conversation with a silly joke. They start talking before they figure out what is going on. This is not because they are bad. It is because no one has taught them to notice first.

This packet is about developing the habit of looking before leaping. You are going to learn to walk into any room — a party, a classroom, a church, a friend's house — and figure out what is happening before you decide how to act. This skill has a name: **calibration**. It means adjusting what you do to fit where you are.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The habit of observing before acting** — pausing to look and listen when entering a new situation
2. **Mood reading** — noticing the emotional tone of a room or group
3. **Volume calibration** — matching your volume to the environment (inside voice, outside voice, and everything between)
4. **Behavioral adjustment** — changing your behavior to fit the setting without being told
5. **Awareness of social leaders** — noticing who is in charge, who is leading, and what the group is doing
6. **Timing awareness** — beginning to understand that when you say or do something matters as much as what you say or do
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Room Talks Before People Do

Every room tells you something before a single person speaks to you. Here is what you can notice:

**Volume level.** Is the room loud or quiet? A loud room means energy, excitement, or chaos. A quiet room means focus, seriousness, or tension. Match the room. Walking into a quiet room at full volume is like playing a trumpet in a library — even if no one tells you to stop, everyone notices.

**Body language of the group.** Are people leaning toward each other (interested, engaged)? Are they sitting back with their arms crossed (closed off, uncomfortable)? Are they moving around (casual, active) or sitting still (formal, focused)?

**Who is in the center?** In every group, someone is the focus of attention. It might be an adult leading a meeting. It might be a child telling a story. It might be someone crying. Noticing who has the group's attention tells you what is happening.

**What is the activity?** Is the group working? Playing? Eating? Talking? Waiting? Each activity has its own rules. You would not start a game of tag during a family dinner. Obvious — but the principle applies everywhere: what is happening here tells you what is appropriate here.

### Part 2: Inside Voice, Outside Voice, and Everything Between

You already know about inside voice and outside voice. But calibration is much more specific than just two settings.

- **Whisper:** When someone near you is sleeping, when you are in a library, when someone is on the phone
- **Soft voice:** When you are in a restaurant, a church, a waiting room, or any place where other groups are also talking and you do not want to disturb them
- **Normal voice:** Regular conversation at home, at a friend's house, in a car
- **Loud voice:** The playground, the backyard, a sporting event, a celebration
- **Projected voice:** When you are speaking to a group, giving a presentation, calling someone who is far away

The skill is not just knowing these levels — it is switching between them automatically when the environment changes. When you walk from the playground into the house, your volume should drop without anyone reminding you. That is calibration.

### Part 3: Watching Before Joining

When you walk up to a group of children who are already playing, the worst thing you can do is jump in without looking. Here is why: you do not know what they are doing yet. You do not know the rules of the game they are playing. You do not know who is on which team or what role is available.

The best strategy is simple:
1. **Walk up and stand nearby.** Not in the middle — on the edge.
2. **Watch for 30 seconds.** Figure out what they are doing and how they are doing it.
3. **Find an entry point.** "Can I play?" or "What are you guys doing?" or simply joining in at a natural break.

This works for adults too. When you walk into a room where adults are talking, do not interrupt. Stand near your parent or another adult you know. Listen. Wait for a pause. Then speak. The child who does this is always welcome. The child who barges in is tolerated but not enjoyed.

### Part 4: Reading Mood

People do not always say how they are feeling. But they almost always show it. Here are signals to watch for:

**Happy/Relaxed:** Smiling, laughing, open body language, animated voices, people moving freely
**Serious/Focused:** Quiet voices, still bodies, concentrated expressions, minimal movement
**Upset/Tense:** Tight faces, crossed arms, sharp voices, people avoiding eye contact, unusual silence
**Excited:** Loud voices, fast movement, big gestures, lots of interrupting (the good kind)

When you notice the mood, you can match it. If everyone is serious, this is not the time for a silly joke. If everyone is excited, this is not the time to complain about being bored. Matching the mood does not mean being fake. It means being aware that you are part of a group, and the group has a feeling right now.

### Part 5: When Is the Right Time?

Timing matters. The exact same question — "Can I have a cookie?" — will get different answers depending on when you ask it.

- Ask when Mom is relaxed and happy: probably yes
- Ask when Mom is on the phone: probably ignored
- Ask when Mom is upset about something: probably no, and possibly snapped at

This is not about manipulation. It is about awareness. A person who pays attention to when people are available, receptive, and in a good mood communicates more effectively than a person who asks for things at random times.

Timing applies to everything: when to ask a question in class, when to join a conversation, when to tell a joke, when to be quiet. The child who develops a sense of timing has a social advantage that lasts a lifetime.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Detective Game [Ongoing — Weekly]
Before entering a new social environment (a party, a store, a church, a friend's house), the student pauses and makes three observations: What is the volume level? What is the mood? What are people doing? The student reports these observations to the mentor. Over time, the student's observations become more specific and accurate.

### Study 2: Volume Calibration Practice [Ongoing]
The mentor and student practice identifying appropriate volume levels for different environments. Visit five different settings in a week (library, restaurant, park, home, store) and after each one, the student identifies what volume was appropriate and why. The mentor notes whether the student adjusted volume naturally or needed reminders.

### Study 3: Watching Before Joining [Practice During Social Events]
At every group social event (playdate, co-op, community gathering), the student practices the watch-then-join strategy. The mentor observes from a distance and debriefs afterward: "What did you notice before you joined? How did you decide when to join?"

### Study 4: Mood Reading Exercises [Bi-Weekly]
Watch a scene from a show or film together. Pause it. Ask the student: "How is this person feeling? How can you tell? What about the other person?" Do the same in real life: "What mood is Dad in right now? How can you tell?"

### Study 5: Timing Experiments [Ongoing]
The student keeps an informal mental log of when requests and questions work well and when they do not. The mentor helps the student identify patterns: "You noticed that asking questions during breakfast works better than right after school. Why do you think that is?"

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Observation Habit
**Developing well:** The child pauses, however briefly, when entering a new environment. They scan the room. They may not articulate what they see, but their behavior adjusts to the setting without prompting.

**Developing slowly:** The child enters every environment at the same energy level. No pause, no scan, no adjustment. They are surprised when corrected.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling the observation pause yourself? Are you playing the detective game regularly? Is the child getting enough practice in varied environments?

### 2. Volume Calibration
**Developing well:** The child adjusts volume for the environment most of the time without reminders. They may still need occasional correction, but the default is calibrated, not maximum.

**Developing slowly:** The child is consistently too loud or too quiet regardless of setting. Reminders do not produce lasting change.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child hearing-impaired? (Rule out physical causes.) Is the household consistently loud, giving the child no model for volume variation? Are you providing enough exposure to quiet environments?

### 3. Mood Reading
**Developing well:** The child can identify basic emotional tones — happy, sad, angry, excited — in individuals and groups. They begin to adjust behavior accordingly (being gentle around someone who is sad, calming down in a tense room).

**Developing slowly:** The child seems oblivious to emotional cues. They joke when someone is crying. They are loud when everyone is tense.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you practiced mood reading explicitly — pointing out emotions in others, asking the child to identify feelings from facial expressions and body language? Some children need explicit instruction in what others pick up intuitively.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Confusing Calibration with Suppression
Calibration is not about making the child smaller, quieter, or less themselves. It is about expanding their range. A child who can be loud at the playground AND quiet at the library has more capability than a child who is loud everywhere or quiet everywhere. If calibration training feels like the child is being constantly shushed, adjust your approach.

### Teaching Only the "Don't" Side
"Don't be loud here" is half the lesson. The other half: "This is a great place to be loud" and "Here is where you can run and yell and be your full self." The child needs to know both when to dial up and when to dial down. Calibration goes both ways.

### Expecting Adult-Level Reading
A five-year-old will not detect that Aunt Margaret is quietly furious because her tone of voice is slightly clipped. Stretching Branches calibration is basic: loud/quiet, happy/sad, active/still. The nuanced reading of social dynamics comes in Weathering Storms. Set appropriate expectations.
